---
title: "Data, plots and descriptive statistics"
subtitle: "Lecture 2"
date: 2026-03-24
webr:
  packages:
    - tidyverse
    - palmerpenguins
    - gt
  render-df: gt-interactive
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Data {background-color="#8e61aa"}

```{webr}
#| edit: false
#| echo: false
#| output: false
options("readr.edition" = 1)
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 20, base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible") + theme(aspect.ratio = 1))
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(gt)
library(patchwork)

theme_set(
  theme_bw(base_size = 20, base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible") +
    theme(aspect.ratio = 1)
)
```

## Data

### Palmer penguins

![](images/lter_penguins.png)

## Data {.scrollable}

### Palmer penguins (observations)

```{r}
obs_table <- function(data, font_size = 22) {
  data |>
    gt() |>
    opt_row_striping() |>
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = font_size,
      table.font.names = "Atkinson Hyperlegible"
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = cells_column_labels()
    )
}

penguins |> obs_table()
```

## Data

### Palmer penguins (variables)

```{r}
penguin_vars <-
  tibble(
    variable = names(penguins),
    description = c(
      "penguin species (Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo)",
      "island where the penguin was found (Biscoe, Dream, Torgersen)",
      "length of bill in mm",
      "depth of bill in mm",
      "length of flipper in mm",
      "body mass in grams",
      "penguin sex (female, male)",
      "year of observation (2007, 2008, 2009)"
    )
  )

vars_table <- function(data, font_size = 30) {
  data |>
    gt() |>
    opt_row_striping() |>
    cols_label(
      variable = md("**Variable**"),
      description = md("**Description**")
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(style = "italic"),
      locations = cells_body(columns = variable)
    ) |>
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = font_size,
      table.font.names = "Atkinson Hyperlegible"
    )
}

penguin_vars |> vars_table()
```

## Data

### Dataframes

- Spreadsheet like arrangement of rows and columns
- When a single row is a unique case (piece of colleted data) = *tidy data*

```{r}
penguins |>
  slice_sample(n = 6) |>
  obs_table()
```

## Data

### Tidy data

![](images/tidydata.png)

- Each variable gets its own column
- Each observation gets its own row
- Each value gets its own cell
- Each new unit of observation gets its own data file

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You measure *enzyme activity* in different tissues (liver, kidney, muscle) from mice at *three* time points (0h, 24h,
  48h). Each tissue sample from each mouse is measured *three* times (to check accuracy), with *absorbance values*
  recorded at 450nm and 600nm.

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You conduct bird surveys across multiple sites over several months. For each survey, you record the *number of
  individuals of different species seen*, the *time spent surveying*, *weather conditions* (temperature, wind, cloud
  cover), and *habitat characteristics* (% tree cover, distance to water).

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Data

### Discussion questions

How would you organise the following data:

- You collect health data from participants in an exercise intervention including their *age*, *height*, *weight* at
  baseline, *weight* after 6 months, *blood pressure* at baseline (systolic/diastolic), *blood pressure* after 6 months
  (systolic/diastolic), and whether they completed the *intervention* (yes/no).

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

# Variable types {background-color="#8e61aa"}

## Variable types

### What is a variable?

- A variable is any property that is measured in an observation [@sokal1995]
- Something that **varies** among things that we can measure [@dytham2011]
- Usually not selected at random, i.e., we should have some interest in it
- Can be related to both a treatment and response in an experiment

## Variable types

### What can we do with variables?

- Summarise how our measurements vary using summary statistics
- Visualise our measurements with figures
- Predict one variable using a second variable
  - The variable we want to predict: **response variable** or **dependant variable**
  - The variable we use to predict our response variable: **explanatory variable** or **independant variable**

## Variable types

### Palmer penguins (variables)

```{r}
penguin_vars_extra <-
  penguin_vars |>
  add_row(
    variable = "cuteness",
    description = "subjective cuteness rating (very low, low, average, high, very high)"
  ) |>
  add_row(
    variable = "parasites",
    description = "number of parasites observed"
  )

penguin_vars_extra |>
  vars_table(font_size = 24)
```

## Variable types

### Palmer penguins (observations)

How would you group these variables? Which are similar kinds of data, and which are different? How many groups would you
make?

```{r}

penguins_extra <-
  penguins |>
  mutate(
    size_score = scale(body_mass_g)[, 1],
    cuteness = case_when(
      size_score < -1.5 ~ "very high",
      size_score < -0.5 ~ "high",
      size_score < 0.5 ~ "average",
      size_score < 1.5 ~ "low",
      TRUE ~ "very low"
    ),
    cuteness = factor(
      cuteness,
      levels = c("very low", "low", "average", "high", "very high")
    ),
    cuteness = if_else(
      row_number() %in% sample(n(), size = floor(n() * 0.05)),
      NA,
      cuteness
    ),
    parasites = rpois(n(), lambda = 3),
    parasites = if_else(
      row_number() %in% sample(n(), size = floor(n() * 0.05)),
      NA_integer_,
      parasites
    )
  ) |>
  select(-size_score)

penguins_extra |>
  slice_sample(n = 8) |>
  obs_table(font_size = 18)
```

{{< countdown minutes=3 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Variable types

### Categorical variables

- Have a fixed number of discrete values
- The measurement we take will assign our observation to a specific value
- E.g., species (*Adelie*, *Chinstrap*, *Gentoo*)
- Can be either **nominal** or **ordinal**
  - **Nominal** variables *do not* have inherent order *(e.g., species)*
  - **Ordinal** variables *do* have an inherent order *(e.g., cuteness)*

## Variable types

### Quantitative variables

- Represented by numbers that reflect a magnitude
- Unlike categorical variables, the numbers we collect mean something tangible
- Can be either **discrete** or **continuous**:
  - **Discrete** variables can only take certain values *(e.g., parasites (1, 2, 3, etc), not 1.55)*
  - **Continuous** variales can take any real number (a number that can be represented by a decimal) within some range.
    *(e.g., bill length or mass)*

# Exploring categorical variables {background-color="#8e61aa"}

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **contingency tables**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows frequency data (how often a given case occurs) for a dataset in a table format.
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins_extra |>
  count(island, species) |>
  obs_table()
```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **contingency tables**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows frequency data (how often a given case occurs) for a dataset in a table format.
- Often shown in "cross-tabs" form for two variables
- Also often shown as either row or column proportion
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins_extra |>
  count(island, species) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = species, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  obs_table()

```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **bar plot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows same informations as acontingency tables
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins_extra |>
  drop_na(sex) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species)) +
  geom_bar()

```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **bar plot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows same informations as acontingency tables
- Can show additional groups
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
bar_plot <-
penguins_extra |>
  drop_na(sex) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, fill = sex)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
bar_plot
```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **bar plot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows same informations as acontingency tables
- Can show additional groups
- Can also be standardised to show proportion

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
bar_plot_fill <-
penguins_extra |>
  drop_na(sex) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, fill = sex)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
bar_plot_fill
```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **bar plot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Shows same informations as acontingency tables
- Can show additional groups
- Can also be standardised to show proportion
- Sometimes showed as "dodged"

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
bar_plot_dodge <-
penguins_extra |>
  drop_na(sex) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, fill = sex)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
bar_plot_dodge
```
:::::
:::

## Exploring categorical variables

### Freqencies: **bar plot**

What information does each format highlight? Which is easiest to read?

```{r}
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-height: 8
bar_plot + bar_plot_fill + bar_plot_dodge + 
plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

{{< countdown minutes=2 font-size="5rem" >}}

# Exploring quantitative variables

## Exploring quantitative variables

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false

penguins_adelie <-
  penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie")

bm_dot <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)") +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  )

bm_dot
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
bm_dot + geom_point(aes(y = 0), fill = "grey25", size = 5) + ylim(0, 10)
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- Variable: body mass of Adelie penguins (grams)
- X axis mapped to body mass
- How best to visualise?

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
bm_dot +
  geom_point(aes(y = 0), size = 5, fill = "grey25") +
  ylim(0, 10) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = seq(
      min(penguins_adelie$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
      max(penguins_adelie$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
      by = 100
    ),
    linewidth = 0.5,
    colour = "red"
  )

```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Distributions: **dotplot**

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- Graphical representation of the distribution of a single variable
- Each observation is represented by a single dot, stacked vertically within bins
- The height of each stack shows the frequency of observations within that range

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false

bm_dot <-
  bm_dot +
  geom_dotplot(
    binwidth = 100,
    colour = "white",
    fill = "grey25",
    method = "histodot"
  )
bm_dot
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Distributions: **histogram**

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- Graphical representation of the distribution of a single variable
- It divides the data into bins (intervals) and displays the frequency of data points in each bin
- The height of each bar represents the number of observations within each bin

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false

bm_hist <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 100, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)")


bm_hist
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Distributions: **dotplots vs histograms**

`binwidth = 100`

```{r}
#| fig-width: 10

bm_dot + bm_hist
```

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Distributions: **dotplots vs histograms**

`binwidth = 50`

```{r}
#| fig-width: 10

bm_hist <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 50, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)")

bm_dot <- 
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)") +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  ) +
  geom_dotplot(
    binwidth = 50,
    colour = "white",
    fill = "grey25",
    method = "histodot"
  )

bm_dot + bm_hist
```

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Distributions: **dotplots vs histograms**

`binwidth = 500`

```{r}
#| fig-width: 10

bm_hist <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 500, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)")

bm_dot <- 
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)") +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  ) +
  geom_dotplot(
    binwidth = 500,
    colour = "white",
    fill = "grey25",
    method = "histodot"
  )

bm_dot + bm_hist
```

{{< countdown minutes=2 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of central tendancy: **the (arithmetic) mean**

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

```{webr}
(6 + 8 + 6 + 2 + 9 + 9 + 7 + 9) / 8
```

```{webr}
mean(c(6, 8, 6, 2, 9, 9, 7, 9))
```

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false

bm_hist <-
  ggplot(
    data = penguins_adelie,
    mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g)
  ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 100, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)")

bm_hist +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(name = "Statistics", values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6")) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.8))
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of central tendancy: **the median**

::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}
- The middle piece of data

```{webr}
data <- c(6, 8, 6, 2, 9, 9, 7, 9)
sort(data)
```

```{webr}
median(data)
```

:::::
::::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| warning: false
bm_hist +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE), y = 0, fill = "Median"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Statistics",
    values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6", "Median" = "#e63946")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.8))
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of central tendancy

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    mean = mean(body_mass_g),
    median = median(body_mass_g)
  )
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguin_hist_all <-
  penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 18, colour = "white", fill = "grey25") +
  facet_wrap(
    vars(species),
    ncol = 1,
    strip.position = "left",
    scales = "free_y"
  ) +
  labs(x = "Body mass (g)", y = "Count") +
  theme(
    aspect.ratio = 0.33,
    strip.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  )

penguin_hist_all +
  geom_point(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(x = body_mass_g, y = 0, fill = "Mean"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(x = body_mass_g, y = 0, fill = "Median"),
    shape = 25,
    colour = "white",
    size = 5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Statistics",
    values = c("Mean" = "#a53ce6", "Median" = "#e63946")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    min = min(body_mass_g),
    max = max(body_mass_g)
  )
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ) |>
      tidyr::pivot_longer(c(min, max), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x"),
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(min = "#1f77b4", max = "#d62728"),
    breaks = c("min", "max"),
    labels = c("Min", "Max"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the range**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- Highly sensitive to outliers

$$
\text{Range} = \text{Max}(x) - \text{Min}(x)
$$

```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    min = min(body_mass_g),
    max = max(body_mass_g)
  ) |>
  mutate(range = max - min)
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ) |>
      tidyr::pivot_longer(c(min, max), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x"),
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        min = min(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        max = max(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    mapping = aes(x = min, xend = max, y = 5, yend = 5),
    arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.1, "inches")),
    colour = "green",
    linewidth = 1,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(min = "#1f77b4", max = "#d62728"),
    breaks = c("min", "max"),
    labels = c("Min", "Max"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **quartiles**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    q1 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.25),
    median = median(body_mass_g),
    q3 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.75)
  )
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
quartiles_df <- penguins |>
  summarise(
    q1 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    q3 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = species
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(q1, median, q3), names_to = "stat", values_to = "x")

penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = quartiles_df,
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(q1 = "#1f77b4", median = "#e63946", q3 = "#2ca02c"),
    labels = c(q1 = "25th", median = "Median", q3 = "75th"),
    name = "Quartiles"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **inter-quartile range (IQR)**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    q1 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.25),
    median = median(body_mass_g),
    q3 = quantile(body_mass_g, 0.75)
  ) |>
  mutate(iqr = q3 - q1)
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = quartiles_df,
    mapping = aes(xintercept = x, colour = stat),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = quartiles_df |> pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = x),
    mapping = aes(x = q1, xend = q3, y = 5, yend = 5),
    arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.1, "inches")),
    colour = "green",
    linewidth = 1,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(q1 = "#1f77b4", median = "#e63946", q3 = "#2ca02c"),
    labels = c(q1 = "25th", median = "Median", q3 = "75th"),
    name = "Quartiles"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the variance**

- The sample variance of a dataset is a measure of the **expected squared distance of data from the mean**

- To calculate:
  - sample size, $N$
  - mean of the sample, $\bar{x}$

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the variance**

- Sample variance ($s^2$)

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

- Sum of squares ($SS$)

$$
SS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the variance**

```{webr}
data <- c(5.5, 4.7, 8.3, 2.1, 9.6, 3.4, 7.7)
```

$$
SS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

$$
SS = (5.5 - 5.9)^2 + (4.7 - 5.9)^2 + (8.3 - 5.9)^2 +\\(2.1 - 5.9)^2 + (9.6 - 5.9)^2 + (3.4 - 5.9)^2 + (7.7 - 5.9)^2
$$

$$
SS = (-0.4)^2 + (-1.2)^2 + (2.4)^2 +\\(-3.8)^2 + (3.7)^2 + (-2.5)^2 + (1.8)^2
$$

$$
SS = 0.16 + 1.44 + 5.76 + 14.44 + 13.69 + 6.25 + 3.24
$$

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the variance**

$$
SS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

$$
SS = 44.98
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{7-1} \times 44.98
$$

$$
s^2 = 7.497
$$

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the variance**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    variance = var(body_mass_g)
  )
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguin_hist_all
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the standard deviation**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    variance = var(body_mass_g)
  ) |>
  mutate(sd = sqrt(variance))
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
sd_hist <-
penguin_hist_all +
  geom_vline(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        mean = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd = sd(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(xintercept = mean),
    colour = "#a53ce6",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_rect(
    data = penguins |>
      summarise(
        mean = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd = sd(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ) |>
      mutate(
        lower = mean - sd,
        upper = mean + sd
      ),
    aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "±1 SD"),
    alpha = 0.2,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("±1 SD" = "#a53ce6")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.9, 0.9))

sd_hist
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the standard deviation**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

```{webr}
penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(
    sd = sd(body_mass_g)
  )
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
sd_hist
```

:::::
:::

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Measures of spread: **the coefficient of variation (CV)**

$$
CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}}
$$

- The standard deviation ($s$) divided by the mean ($\bar{x}$)
- Becomes unitless / dimensionless
- Useful for comparing spread across datasets with very different means
  - E.g. variability in lengths of tails in mice and dogs
- Often presented as a percentage

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Descriptions of shape: **skew**

- The asymmetry of the distribution

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 12
# Create two datasets with different skews
set.seed(1234)
# More intense left skew
left_skewed <- rbeta(1000, 20, 0.5) * 100

# More intense right skew
right_skewed <- rbeta(1000, 0.5, 20) * 100
left_skewed <- rbeta(1000, 15, 1) * 100
no_skew <- rnorm(1000, 50, 3)

# Combine into a single dataframe
skewed_data <- tibble(
  value = c(left_skewed, no_skew, right_skewed),
  skew = rep(
    c("Left / negative skew", "No skew (normal)", "Right / positive skew"),
    each = 1000
  )
)

means <- tibble(
  value = c(mean(left_skewed), mean(no_skew), mean(right_skewed)),
  skew = c("Left / negative skew", "No skew (normal)", "Right / positive skew")
)

medians <- tibble(
  value = c(median(left_skewed), median(no_skew), median(right_skewed)),
  skew = c("Left / negative skew", "No skew (normal)", "Right / positive skew")
)

# Plot using ggplot with facets
ggplot(skewed_data, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "grey60", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(data = means, aes(xintercept = value, color = "Mean")) +
  geom_vline(data = medians, aes(xintercept = value, color = "Median")) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name = "Statistic",
    values = c("Mean" = "purple", "Median" = "red")
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~skew, scales = "free") +
  theme(
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top"
  )

```

## Exploring quantitative variables

### Descriptions of shape: **modality**

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
set.seed(42)
bimodal_data <- tibble(
  value = c(rnorm(500, mean = 30, sd = 8), rnorm(500, mean = 60, sd = 8)),
  distribution = "Bimodal"
)

# Visualize
ggplot(bimodal_data, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "grey60", color = "white") +
  labs() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank())
```

# Relationships between variables {background-color="#8e61aa"}

## Relationships between variables

- Many research questions wish to answer if two variables are related:
  - Does tree spacing affect timber yield?
  - Does egg mass affect offspring survival?
  - Do face masks reduce transmission of SARS-CoV-2?
  - Do heavier penguins also have longer bills?

## Relationships between variables

### Two quantitative variables

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{r}
#| fig-align: center
bill_scatter <-
  penguins_extra |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = bill_length_mm)) +
  labs(
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Bill Length (mm)"
  )

bill_scatter
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
- Do heavier penguins also have longer bills?

:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Two quantitative variables

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{r}
#| fig-align: center

bill_scatter + geom_point(y = 0, alpha = 0.7)
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
- Do heavier penguins also have longer bills?
- Map X axis to body mass

:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Two quantitative variables: **scatterplot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{r}
#| fig-align: center

bill_scatter + geom_point(alpha = 0.7)
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
- Do heavier penguins also have longer bills?
- Map X axis to body mass
- Map Y axis to bill length

:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Two quantitative variables: **scatterplot**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
```{r}
#| fig-align: center
bill_scatter + geom_point(aes(colour =species), alpha = 0.7) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

:::::
::::: {.column}
- Do heavier penguins also have longer bills?
- Map X axis to body mass
- Map Y axis to bill length
- Colour by category

:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Quantitative variables by category

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.1, width = 0.3)
```
:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Quantitative variables by category: **boxplots**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- **Box** shows the interquartile range (IQR):
  - Bottom of box = 25th percentile (Q1)
  - Line inside box = median (50th percentile)
  - Top of box = 75th percentile (Q3)
- **Whiskers** typically to 1.5 × IQR beyond the quartiles
- Points beyond the whiskers are potential **outliers**
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.1, width = 0.3) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = NA)
```
:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### Quantitative variables by category: **violin plots**

::: {.columns}
::::: {.column}
- **Width** of the violin shows the density of observations at that value
- Wider sections indicate more observations at that body mass
- The shape reveals the distribution of the data
:::::
::::: {.column}
```{r}
penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.1, width = 0.3) +
  geom_violin(fill = NA)
```
:::::
:::

## Relationships between variables

### How to quantify relationships?

```{r}
penguins |>
  filter(species != "Adelie") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.1, width = 0.3) +
  geom_violin(fill = NA)
```

{{< countdown minutes=2 font-size="5rem" >}}

## Relationships between variables

### How to quantify relationships?

```{r}
penguins |>
  filter(species != "Adelie") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.1, width = 0.3) +
  geom_violin(fill = NA) +
  geom_point(
    data = penguins |>
      filter(species != "Adelie") |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    size = 5,
    colour = "red"
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = penguins |>
      filter(species != "Adelie") |>
      summarise(
        body_mass_g = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
      ),
    aes(group = 1),
    colour = "red",
    linewidth = 1
  )
```

## Relationships between variables

### How to quantify relationships?

- Compare statistics between groups
  - difference in means
  - difference in medians
  - difference in variances

## Relationships between variables

### How to quantify relationships?

```{r}
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_point()
```

## Relationships between variables

### How to quantify relationships?

```{r}
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  stat_ellipse(level = 0.95)
```

## Reading

📖 [IMS2](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/) [[1]](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/data-hello)
[[4]](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/explore-categorical) [[5]](https://openintro-ims.netlify.app/explore-numerical)

## References
